retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the development of a fetus, the head and neck region partially develops from the brachial apparatus. The brachial apparatus is broken down into branchial clefts, pouches, and arches. The clefts are made up of ectoderm, but only the first cleft will form the external acoustic meatus while the rest of the clefts will obliterate. The brachial arch is composed of mesoderm and neural crest. The arches will eventually divide into the arteries, muscles, cartilage, and bony structures in the face and neck. The brachial pouches are made up of endoderm that will develop into the organs in the head and neck. Along with the development of the structures in the head and neck, there are aortic arches. The aortic arches will eventually develop into arteries for the head, face, neck, and branches from the aorta. The first aortic arch is responsible for developing the maxillary artery, which is one of the terminal branches from the external carotid artery. The second branch is the stapedial artery, which comes off the posterior auricular artery in the adult. The third aortic arch will form the common carotid artery, which will eventually fuse with the superior branches and become the main artery that supplies blood to the superior arteries derived from first and second aortic arches. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Ascending Palatine Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of the ascending palatine artery in utero begins during the 4th and 5th weeks. The aortic arches arise from the aortic sac which allows for the development of the external carotid artery from the 3rd arch, which gives rise to the facial artery which later gives rise to the ascending palatine artery. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Studies show that a portion of the right primitive ventral aorta between the 4th and 6th PAAs forms the brachiocephalic trunk and a part of the aortic arch. The common origin of the right 3rd and 4th PAAs also elongate to form the brachiocephalic trunk. The right subclavian artery persists from the right 4th PAA's proximal part, while the left 4th PAA contributes to the left common carotid artery (CCA). The right CCA develops from the brachiocephalic trunk, and new branches from the 3rd PAAs' ventral aspects form the ECAs, along with portions of the 1st and 2nd arch arteries. The 3rd PAA transforms into the carotid sinus and forms the internal carotid artery (ICA) with the cranial dorsal aorta. Meanwhile, the cranial ventral aorta becomes the ECA, which gives rise to the facial artery. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The nerve innervation of the face will also originate from the first and second pharyngeal arches. The first pharyngeal arch will form the trigeminal nerve, while the second pharyngeal arch develops the facial nerve. As for the blood supply to the face, the third aortic arch forms the common carotid artery, which divided into the internal and external carotid arteries as it elongates. As the external carotid artery elongates, it further branches into the superior thyroid, lingual, facial, ascending pharyngeal, occipital, posterior auricular, and superficial temporal arteries. Uniquely, the first aortic arch forms the maxillary artery that perfuses the deeper structures of the face. The maxillary anastomoses with the external carotid artery becoming one of the terminal arterial branches of the external carotid artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Posterior Auricular Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During embryology, the arteries in the face, neck, and chest form from the aortic arches. The aortic arches will form around the same time as the formation brachial apparatus. The third aortic arch will develop into the common carotid artery. The common carotid artery will ascend laterally along the face, neck, and head. At the level of cervical vertebra four, the common carotid artery will bifurcate into the external carotid artery and internal carotid artery. The external carotid artery is responsible for perfusing the face and head. One of the branches that come off the external carotid artery is the posterior auricular artery. The external carotid artery branches into the posterior auricular artery at the level of the angle of the mandible. The posterior artery will then ascend towards the region posterior to the ear.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the 4th and 5th weeks of embryological development, the aortic sac gives rise to the aortic arches. The external carotid artery develops from the 3rd aortic arch and gives rise to the lingual artery. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Human arterial development begins around the 19th day of gestation, with longitudinal channels forming paired dorsal aortae. The heart tubes fuse by days 21 to 25, and the ventral aortic sac remains connected to the dorsal aortae via the 1st primitive aortic arch (PAA). The 1st and 2nd PAAs disappear on day 29. By day 32, 6 pairs of PAAs are present. Regression between the 3rd and 4th PAAs occurs around 6 weeks or at an embryo size of 12 to 14 mm.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Branchial Arches -- Function -- Fourth branchial arch:
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft: cervical sinus Arch: thyroid/cuneiform cartilages, CN X, aortic arch, right subclavian artery, various laryngeal muscles Pouch: apex of piriform sinus, superior parathyroid glands Nerve: vagus nerve (CN X), superior laryngeal nerve

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lateral Nasal Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the fourth and fifth weeks of embryological development, the aortic arches form parallel with the branchial arches and ultimately develop into the arteries of the face, neck, and chest. The third aortic arch develops into the common carotid artery, which branches into the external and internal carotid arteries. [7] The external carotid artery gives off the facial artery, from which the anterior lateral nasal artery branches. The posterior lateral nasal artery is a branch of the sphenopalatine artery, itself a branch of the internal maxillary artery, which derives from the first aortic arch and is the primary artery of the first branchial arch; it ultimately joins with the external carotid artery to perfuse the deep structures of the face. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Part of the maxillary artery arises from the remnant of the first aortic arch. In general the first and second part of the aortic arch regress and do not remain to form into any permanent anatomical structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Artery -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The intra-embryonic vessels originate from blood islands in the splanchnic mesoderm in front of the protocordal disc, where a plexiform horseshoe-shaped network forms surrounding the cephalic portion of the embryo. At the same time, two groups of angioblastic cells, in a dorsal paramedian position, form two parallel vessels, the dorsal aorta, which connect cephalically connect to the plexiform network. The further development of the vascular system consists of the formation of larger caliber ducts with a primitive plexiform network. The most voluminous of these are the right and left primitive aortas, from which the fetal arterial tree originates and develops parallel to the heart tube and its subsequent evolution.The formation of an anastomosis between the intra- and extra-embryonic vessels completes the circulatory system.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Laryngeal Nerves -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The differences in the right and left RLNs' courses are due to the aortic arches' embryological derivatives. The 4th aortic arch typically forms the arch of the aorta on the left, while the right contributes in some part to the right subclavian artery's formation. The subclavian arteries develop from the right and left 7th cervical intersegmental arteries. The 6th aortic arch forms the pulmonary artery on the right and the pulmonary artery and ductus arteriosus (future ligamentum arteriosum) on the left. The right 6th aortic arch's dorsal segment disappears.

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Aortic Arch Ring -- Etiology -- Embryological Origin
retrieved_docs.content: Right horn - brachiocephalic artery Left horn - proximal aortic arch 3rd arch - common carotid artery, external carotid artery and the proximal portion of the internal carotid artery on both the sides Left 4th arch - arch between the left common carotid artery (LCCA) and left subclavian artery (LSCA) Right 4th arch - proximal portion of right subclavian artery (RSCA) Left sixth arch - proximal portion forms the left pulmonary artery (LPA). The distal portion forms the patent ductus arteriosus (PDA). Right sixth arch - proximal portion forms the right pulmonary artery (RPA). The distal portion disappears. Right dorsal aorta - right distal subclavian artery Left dorsal aorta - left aortic arch distal to LSCA

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Aorta -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The aortic arch forms through the development of the branchial arch arteries. There are six branchial arch arteries, also known as the pharyngeal arch vessels. These develop into the following [2] [3] : The first branchial arch artery forms the maxillary and external carotid arteries The second branchial arch artery leads to the formation of the stapedial arteries The third branchial arch artery contributes to the formation of the right common carotid arteries and proximal internal carotid artery The fourth branchial arch artery forms the main portion of the aortic arch, as well as the proximal right subclavian artery - the left subclavian artery arises from the left seventh intersegmental artery, which describes a set of arteries that develop from the dorsal aorta The fifth branchial arch does not form vessels and regresses The sixth branchial arch leads to the formation of the main pulmonary artery, left and right pulmonary artery, and ductus arteriosus

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Branchial Arches -- Function -- Third branchial arch:
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft: cervical sinus Arch: the body of the hyoid, greater horn of hyoid, superior constrictor muscles, internal carotid arteries, CNIX Pouch: thymus, piriform recess, inferior parathyroid glands Nerve: glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pharynx Muscles -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Embryologically these muscles are derived from the branchial arches that form during the fourth through seventh weeks of gestation. Their blood supply and innervation correlate with the arch from which they derive. The STP is derived from the third branchial arch, while the rest of the pharyngeal muscles arise from the fourth pharyngeal arch. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1681
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta is a central arterial structure whose description may include several allowed qualifiers: its anatomy and histology, its innervation, and its growth and development. These qualifiers provide comprehensive context for understanding the vessel’s structural and functional characteristics. In clinical practice, the aorta serves as the direct procedure site for surgical interventions such as aortic resection, replacement, or anastomosis, highlighting its importance in cardiovascular surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 785
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic procedures for arteries (excluding the aorta) form a broad category that includes a variety of surgical and interventional techniques targeting specific arterial structures. Within this category, استئصال باطنة الشريان السباتي (excision of the inner lining of the carotid artery) is classified as a child procedure, as are تطويق الشريان الرئوي (pulmonary artery banding), استئصال باطنة الشريان الرئوي (excision of the inner lining of the pulmonary artery), استئصال العصيدة (excision of aneurysmal tissue), تدخل للأوعية الدموية عن طريق الجلد (percutaneous vascular intervention), and مجازة الشريان التاجي (coronary artery bypass). The arteria pulmonalis (pulmonary artery) is directly involved in تطويق الشريان الرئوي, making it the direct procedural site for pulmonary artery banding, while it serves as an indirect procedural site for استئصال باطنة الشريان الرئوي, indicating that the inner‑lining excision affects the pulmonary artery without being performed directly on it. Additionally, pulmonary artery catheter insertion performed via the femoral vein, jugular vein, or subclavian vein each has the arteria pulmonalis as an indirect procedure site, reflecting that these catheterization techniques ultimately engage the pulmonary artery through a vascular access route. Together, these relationships illustrate how arterial therapeutic procedures are organized by target vessel and by the nature of their interaction (direct versus indirect) with the pulmonary artery, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and planning arterial interventions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a child component, indicating that the basilar artery is a sub‑structure within the broader intracranial arterial system. This anatomical structure is identified as a finding site for تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting its clinical relevance in the assessment of cerebrovascular disease. Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is described as an indirect procedure site of the Structure of intracranial artery, meaning that surgical removal of the arterial intima in head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed in relation to this intracranial arterial region. The procedure Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship with استئصال باطنة الشريان (general endarterectomy), placing the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy as a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Finally, استئصال باطنة الشريان has member cases classified under حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases), indicating that endarterectomy is a therapeutic option for patients presenting with arterial thrombotic or embolic events. Together, these relationships delineate how the intracranial arterial anatomy, associated pathological findings, and endarterectomy procedures are interconnected in the diagnosis and management of cerebrovascular and arterial thrombo‑embolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction caused by embolism of the precerebral arteries, by thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, or by occlusion of the basilar artery is described with the morphological hallmark الاحتشاء (infarction), a finding that is also linked to the cardiac entity احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ تَحْتَ الشِّغاف (heart‑muscle infarction). الاحتشاء is in turn related to انسداد الأوعية الدموية (vascular occlusion), emphasizing that tissue infarction typically follows an occlusive vascular event. Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion is mapped to occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, which is identified as the upstream source of both embolic and thrombotic precerebral arterial infarcts and as the direct cause of basilar‑artery infarction; thus, occlusion/stenosis of precerebral arteries serves as a central mechanistic node linking these stroke entities. Within the broader nosology, the category Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System is a child of transient cerebral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: situates these processes in the basilar artery (Arteria basilaris) and the broader Structure of intracranial artery, which serves as a finding site for occlusion/stenosis, transient cerebral ischemia, and intracranial atherosclerosis, and as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of head and neck vessels (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) under the broader procedure category endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان). This integrated network supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial disease, its neurologic manifestations, and systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1892
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall intracranial arterial architecture. This anatomical framework also serves as a common site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting the importance of evaluating the basilar artery and related intracranial vessels for sclerotic changes that can impact cerebral blood flow and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 986
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic procedures for arteries (excluding the aorta) — الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالشرايين (باستثناء الأورطي) — serve as a hierarchical category that encompasses a variety of specific surgical and interventional techniques. Within this category, internal carotid artery excision — استئصال باطنة الشريان السباتي — and the more general internal artery excision — استئصال باطنة الشريان — are listed as child procedures, indicating that they are specialized forms of arterial treatment. Endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels — endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck — is further classified as a subtype of internal artery excision, reflecting its role in removing atherosclerotic material from the arterial wall. Pulmonary‑artery–related interventions are also integrated into this framework. Pulmonary artery encirclement — تطويق الشريان الرئوي — and pulmonary artery interior excision — استئصال باطنة الشريان الرئوي — are both children of the arterial therapeutic procedures category,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: and peripheral/visceral atherosclerosis, all of which contribute to the risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, illustrating the systemic impact of vascular disease on cerebral blood flow. These entities are organized hierarchically: general cardiac and vascular diagnoses and risk factors feed into specific diagnostic categories; diagnostic categories give rise to biopsy techniques; biopsy techniques serve as parents for detailed ventricular or pericardial biopsy procedures; biopsies and excisions connect to broader operative groups such as “HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES” and “other or heart procedures.” This integrated framework delineates the relationships among anatomical structures (heart, pericardium, papillary muscles), diagnostic modalities, therapeutic interventions, and related disease conditions, supporting comprehensive cardiac and vascular patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: procedure site, highlighting the relevance of these venous access routes for procedures that ultimately involve the pulmonary artery. The Structure of intracranial artery has a finding site of تصلب عصيدي في المخ, signifying the presence of intracranial atherosclerosis, and it is an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck. This relationship suggests that endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels can affect intracranial arterial structures, linking vascular surgical interventions to intracranial vascular pathology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how a hierarchical set of arterial therapeutic interventions, catheterization techniques, and vascular findings are interconnected within the broader framework of vascular medicine, providing clinicians with a comprehensive view of procedural options and their anatomical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1077
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta is a central arterial vessel that is directly linked to the visual representation titled “صورة الأبهر,” indicating that an image of the aorta is an important reference. In both anatomical and histological contexts, the aorta is recognized as an “Allowed qualifier,” meaning its structural and tissue characteristics are explicitly considered when describing or studying the vessel. Additionally, the aorta’s innervation is also listed under the “Allowed qualifier” category, highlighting that its nerve supply is a relevant attribute for clinical and anatomical evaluation. Surgical interventions such as aortic resection, replacement, or anastomosis are performed on the aorta, making the aorta the “direct_procedure_site_of” for these procedures. This integrated view underscores the aorta’s significance in imaging, anatomical‑histological description, neural innervation, and as the primary site for major vascular surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular cannula insertion is a specific type of Procedure on blood vessel and therefore falls under the broader category تروية دموية (blood perfusion). Within this hierarchy, Procedure on blood vessel is itself classified as a kind of تروية دموية and is placed under the parent category “other or procedures on vessels of head and neck.” Vascular cannula insertion has a child procedure named قثطرة الشريان الرئوي (pulmonary artery catheterization), indicating a more detailed sub‑procedure within the same family. The procedural site for these interventions is linked to blood perfusion, with Vascular cannula insertion having an indirect procedure‑site relationship, while Procedure on blood vessel is directly associated with the procedure‑site concept. This integrated classification clarifies how vascular cannulation techniques relate to each other, to the anatomical region of the vessels, and to the underlying physiological process of blood perfusion, supporting coherent understanding and management of vascular procedures in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1198
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery is identified as the anatomical site where تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis) can be found. In addition, this intracranial arterial structure serves as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of vessels of the head and neck, indicating that surgical removal of plaque from head‑and‑neck arteries may be relevant to or impact the intracranial arterial anatomy.
